topic: I service vending machines for a living and the route binder for a machine in a town I have never worked in has my own handwriting and signatures going back fourteen years
title: I Found a Vending Machine Route Binder Signed in My Own Handwriting. I Have Never Been to That Town. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I have done vending machine maintenance for twenty years across four states. Last March I drove a substitution route into a town in central Ohio called Latham Hollow. The post office machine had a route binder taped to the side. Eight hundred and forty service signatures going back fourteen years. All of them mine. I had never been to Latham Hollow.
description:
I have done vending machine maintenance for twenty years across four states. Last March I drove a substitution route into a town in central Ohio called Latham Hollow. The post office machine had a route binder taped to the side. Eight hundred and forty service signatures going back fourteen years. All of them mine. I had never been to Latham Hollow.

The binders we use are simple. Date, time, signature, repair notes. A district manager spot checks them quarterly against the master schedule. The Latham Hollow binder matched every quirk of my handwriting. The way I cross my sevens. The way I press harder on the down stroke than the up stroke. The coffee ring on page nineteen. My wife confirmed the signatures were mine.

There is a detail about what I saw at the end of the unpaved fire road that I have only told one person about. The shop in town is still on my permanent route. Watch to the end. The binder is waiting at the machine. Next Thursday's entry is already signed.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundfootagestories
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I have done vending machine maintenance for twenty years. The work is mostly mechanical. Bill validators, coil motors, refrigeration units, the occasional rat in a cabinet. We carry a binder for each machine on our route. Service signature, date, time, repair notes. The binders live in a metal box at the side of the machine. Last March I drove into a town in central Ohio called Latham Hollow on a substitution route. I opened the binder for the machine in the post office lobby. My name was inside it. Eight hundred and forty service signatures. Going back fourteen years. I had never been to Latham Hollow.

close shot of a man's work boots and lower leg crouched on a tile lobby floor in front of a vending machine, only the legs visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: The vending company I work for is regional. We cover four states. Each technician is assigned a fixed route of about sixty machines. You work the same machines for years. The binders are inspected quarterly. A district manager checks the signatures against the master schedule. Latham Hollow is not on my route. Latham Hollow has not been on any route I have worked for the entire fourteen years that binder covers. The substitution that day was a favor for a technician with the stomach flu. I drove an hour and forty minutes east of my territory. I had never heard of the town. There is one stoplight. There is one post office.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a service van with a vending company logo parked outside a small office building at dawn, no driver visible, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a printed paper route schedule on a clipboard with town names and machine ID numbers, one row circled in red pen, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide exterior of a small town intersection with a single stoplight hanging on a wire, no traffic, late morning light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: My handwriting has not changed since I started this job. I sign my last name in full and then write the time using a colon between the hour and minutes. I use a felt tip pen because ballpoints skip when you are crouched at floor level. I draw a small box around the word coil if I worked on the coil motor that day. I draw a circle around the word bill if I worked on the validator. There are technicians on our team who have copied this style over the years. There are not many. The binder in Latham Hollow was written entirely in this style.
image_prompts: close detail of a black felt tip pen resting on a handwritten service log page, a small drawn box visible around the word coil, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of an open binder page showing entries with circled and boxed words in the notes column, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a stack of identical felt tip pens in the door pocket of a service van, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I sat on the floor of the post office lobby and read the binder cover to cover. Eight hundred and forty entries. Three a year for fourteen years, on a rough schedule. Replacements of the coil motor in two thousand and twelve. A refrigeration unit recharge in two thousand and seventeen. A bill validator swap last August. The dates were specific. The mistakes I tend to make were in the entries. I write the digit four with an open top. I cross my sevens. I sometimes misspell the word fluorescent. Every quirk of my handwriting was in that binder. There was a coffee ring on page nineteen that I recognized.
image_prompts: wide interior of a post office lobby with a man sitting on the floor reading an open binder beside a vending machine, only seen from behind, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a binder page showing handwritten entries dated through twenty seventeen, the page slightly yellowed, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a binder page with the word fluorescent visibly misspelled in the notes column of an entry, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a circular coffee ring stain on the corner of a service log page, faint brown discoloration, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of an open binder spread across knees on a tiled floor, both pages dense with handwriting, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: My first thought was that another technician on our team shares my last name. There are two of us with the surname Beals. The other Beals works out of the Pittsburgh office. He could not have driven to central Ohio for fourteen years without district payroll noticing. My second thought was that I had been there once and forgotten. People forget years of their lives. I did not forget fourteen years. My third thought was that someone had forged my signature. The forgery would have to predate my hiring. The first entry in the binder was dated September of two thousand and nine. I started this job in November of two thousand and nine.

wide shot of a man at his kitchen table at night staring at a laptop screen, seen from behind, only the back of the head visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide interior of a quiet suburban kitchen at night, the binder open on the table, dim overhead light, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I took photographs of every page. I drove home and laid the photographs out on the kitchen table next to my personal logbook from my own route. The handwriting matched at every level. The pressure on the pen. The leftward slant. The way I press harder on the down stroke than the up stroke. My wife came in halfway through and asked what I was looking at. I told her. She walked across the room and looked at the photographs. She said the signatures were mine. She has known me since high school. She knows my handwriting better than I do. She also said the coffee ring on page nineteen was mine.

sections.narration: I called the post office in Latham Hollow on Monday. I asked the postmaster how long the vending machine had been in the lobby. He told me it had been there since the building was renovated in two thousand and nine. He told me the same technician had serviced it for as long as he could remember. A tall man, brown hair, blue work shirt with the company patch. He came on Thursdays at three in the afternoon. I am six foot one with brown hair. I wear a blue work shirt. I service my regular route on Thursdays. The postmaster said the technician had been there last Thursday. I had been at home last Thursday.
image_prompts: close shot of a corded landline phone on a kitchen counter, the receiver lifted and resting on a notepad, dim afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide exterior of a small brick post office with a sign reading LATHAM HOLLOW in late afternoon light, no people, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a blue work shirt with an embroidered company patch hanging on the back of a chair, horizontal 16:9 framing

close shot of a wall calendar in a kitchen with last Thursday's square marked with a domestic errand in pencil, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I drove back to Latham Hollow the next Thursday at exactly three. I parked across the street from the post office. I sat in the car and watched the front door. At three fourteen a man walked out of the building. He was carrying a brown service binder. He wore a blue work shirt with the company patch. He was the same height as me. He had brown hair. He walked to a white panel van parked in the back lot. The van had no markings. The license plate did not match any vehicle our fleet has ever owned. He did not look toward me. He drove west out of town toward the county line.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a small town main street viewed from inside a parked car at three in the afternoon, the post office across the road, horizontal 16:9 framing
wide shot of a man in a blue work shirt walking out of a brick post office carrying a binder, seen from a great distance, no face visible, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of an unmarked white panel van parked in a gravel back lot of a small town building, no logos on the side, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide exterior of a county road heading west out of a small town at late afternoon, a white van visible as a small dot in the distance, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I followed him. I kept four car lengths back the way I do when I am tailing a slow shipment. He drove fifteen miles into the woods on a county route I had never been on. He turned onto an unpaved fire road at a sign that said no outlet. I parked at the head of the road and walked in on foot. The van was parked at the end of the road in a clearing. There was no house. There was no building. There was a single chain link cage about the size of a closet, set on a concrete pad. He was sitting inside it on a folding chair. He looked up at me through the wire. He had my face.

sections.narration: I walked back to my car. I drove home. I did not tell my wife. I did not tell my district manager. I requested a permanent reassignment off the substitution roster the following Monday. The district manager called me into the office and told me he had no record of me ever being assigned to Latham Hollow. The Thursday after that, my regular schedule changed. The post office in Latham Hollow has been added to my permanent route. The work order was signed by a manager who retired in two thousand and twelve. The binder is waiting at the machine. The entries continue past last Thursday. There is one for next Thursday. The signature is already there.
